content.full_text:
FPPC OPINIONS

BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

A-75-12-007

In the Matter of:

Opinion requested by
Mayor Janet Gray Hayes)
Dinner Committee

)
)
)
)
)

No. 75-145
December 4, 1975

BY THE COMMISSION: We have been asked the following
question by the Mayor Janet Gray Hayes Dinner Committee:

The Mayor Janet Gray Hayes Dinner Committee sponsored a dinner on April 17, 1975, to raise funds for the
non-reimbursable expenses of the Mayor's office. Seven
hundred tickets were sold at $20 apiece and the proceeds
will be used to hire a part-time assistant who will work
to maintain the organization which handled the Mayor's
campaign, to publish a newsletter, and to cover non-reimbursable travel, dinner and postage expenses, among other
items.

Do the above-described uses of the proceeds of
such a fundraising dinner disqualify those proceeds from
being treated solely as campaign contributions and expenditures reportable under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform
Act and make these proceeds "income" within the meaning of
Section 82030, which is reportable by the Mayor under Chapter 7 of the Act?

CONCLUSION

The proceeds from the fundraising dinner retain
their classification solely as campaign contributions and
expenditures so long as they are used to support those
activities related to the Mayor's responsibilities as an
elected official and a future candidate.

No. 75-145
Page Two

1 FPPC OPINIONS

ANALYSIS

We are concerned here with whether funds received
by an elected official's campaign committee, and subsequently
expended for various purposes, retain their classification
as campaign contributions and expenditures reportable only
under Chapter 4, Government Code Sections 84200, et seq.,1/
or become income to the elected official which is reportable
by the official under Chapter 7, Sections 87200, et seq.2/
The specific purposes for which those funds received through
the dinner sponsored by the Mayor Janet Gray Hayes Dinner
Committee will be used are:

(1) Hire a part-time assistant who will work to
maintain the organization which supported the Mayor's cam-
paign;

(2) Publish a newsletter from the Mayor;

(3) Pay non-reimbursable travel expenses of the
Mayor to conventions, conferences and meetings;

(4) Purchase Mayor's trophies and awards;

(5) Pay dinner expenses and travel costs not
covered by the City of San Jose;

(6) Pay travel expenses, postage and other expenses
of the Mayor's volunteer assistants; and

(7) Provide honoraria for future speakers for fund-
raisers.

Those activities listed in (1), (2), (3), (4), (6)
and (7) appear to be related to the Mayor's responsibilities
as an elected official and future candidate, and have the
effect of influencing future voter action. Accordingly,
funds expended for these activities may properly be treated
as campaign expenditures reportable by the Dinner Committee
under Chapter 4 of the Political Reform Act. See opinion
requested by Willie L. Brown, 1 FPPC Opinions 67 (No. 75-055,
July 2, 1975).

1/
All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

2/
We assume, for purposes of this opinion, that the
dinner was a political fundraiser and that the purchase of
a ticket, therefore, was a contribution. See Section 82015.

No. 75-145
Page Three

FPPC OPINIONS

Funds expended for category (5), on the other hand,
may or may not be campaign expenditures reportable only
under Chapter 4. If the expenses are incurred in connection
with Ms. Hayes' political or governmental activities, they
can be treated in the same manner as expenses incurred for
the other activities specified herein. If, however, the expenses are incurred to support personal dinner and travel
costs of the Mayor, they will constitute a gift to the Mayor
from the Dinner Committee. Since gifts are included within
the definition of income, Section 82030, the expenses will be
reportable by the Dinner Committee and also will be reported
by the Mayor on her Statement of Economic Interests filed
pursuant to Chapter 7 of the Act (assuming, of course, that
the requisite amount of $25.00 or more is involved). Sections
87203, 87207.

Approved by the Commission on December 4, 1975.
Concurring: Brosnahan, Carpenter, Lowenstein and Miller.
Commissioner Waters was absent.

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman
